narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after pouring so much into your law school dreams, and I want to walk you through why our prediction model saw a 65% chance of you struggling to pass the bar exam, leading to this tough call on your application. Picture it like a story of patterns the model has learned from thousands of students like you: your Year 3 grades landing you in the bottom decile of your school stood out as the biggest red flag, whispering that the rigors of advanced legal studies might prove an uphill battle. That low decile built on a similar story from your first year, where you also ranked in the 1st decile, suggesting those early habits or challenges could carry forward into bar prep. Your LSAT score of 30 echoed this, pulling strongly toward the risk of falling short on the high-stakes exam that demands quick, precise thinking under pressure. With your undergraduate GPA at a solid 3.5, things looked steadier before law school, but it still nudged the outlook downward in this mix. Your middle family income added another layer, as the model sees students without extra financial cushions sometimes stretching thin during intense study phases. Being a white female full-time student rounded out the picture, with those traits historically linking to hurdles in passing rates among past applicants. It's not that any one piece defines you—your full-time commitment shows real drive—but together, they painted a profile where the model foresaw too many headwinds for bar success. This isn't a judgment on your potential or heart; it's just the model's honest read based on what similar paths have shown before. You've got strengths here, like that respectable UGPA, that could shine with targeted changes, and many students pivot from early stumbles to thrive. Hang in there—law might still be yours with a few adjustments to those grade trends. We're rooting for your next chapter.
